lessons.title: Welcome to SRVY 1341 — Land Surveying
learning_objectives: Describe the scope of SRVY 1341 and how traversing ties field measurement to computed boundaries.
Identify the major topics of the course: measurement, angles, traverse adjustment, COGO, area, and boundary principles.
Recognize the Texas regulatory context (TBPELS, GLO) that frames professional land surveying.
key_takeaways: SRVY 1341 is a core land-surveying course built around the closed traverse: measure, check, adjust, and compute coordinates and area.
Topics progress from distance and angle measurement, through traverse adjustment and coordinate geometry, to boundary determination and subdivision.
Texas surveying is regulated by the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS) and rooted in the General Land Office (GLO) original-survey system — no PLSS grid.
Accuracy, redundant observations, and a defensible record are the habits this course builds.

content.html:
What this course is about

Welcome to SRVY 1341 — Land Surveying. This is a core, computation-heavy course in the determination of boundaries, areas, shapes, and locations through traversing. You will learn to measure distances and angles in the field, carry direction through a connected series of lines, detect and quantify error, adjust the work so it closes, and from the adjusted data compute coordinates and area. Those computed results become the numbers in a legal description and on a plat.

The unifying idea is the closed traverse: a connected loop (or link between known points) whose measurements can be mathematically checked. Everything else in the course either feeds the traverse (measurement, angles) or flows from it (coordinate geometry, area, boundary location).

How the weeks fit together

The semester builds in a deliberate order. Early weeks cover distance measurement (chaining and tape corrections) and direction (compass surveying, declination, angle types and measurement). The middle weeks turn to the heart of the course: latitudes and departures, error of closure and precision ratios, and the compass (Bowditch) and transit adjustment rules. After the midterm we apply the adjusted data through coordinate geometry (inversing and intersections) and area computation (coordinate and DMD methods), then study boundary determination, retracement, subdivision and curves, and professional standards.

content.text: Bring a non-programmable and a programmable calculator. The Texas surveyor-in-training (SIT) and RPLS exams reward disciplined, step-by-step traverse computations — exactly the workflow this course drills.

Texas context: Professional land surveying in Texas is regulated by the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS) under Tex. Occ. Code ch. 1071, with standards of practice in 22 Texas Administrative Code ch. 138. Texas land descends from original General Land Office (GLO) surveys keyed to survey name + abstract number — Texas has no federal PLSS township-range grid.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The course centers on the closed traverse: measure, check, adjust, compute coordinates and area.
Weeks progress from measurement and direction, through adjustment, to coordinate geometry, area, and boundary work.
Texas surveying is regulated by TBPELS and rooted in the GLO original-survey system (no PLSS).
Redundant observations and a defensible record are the professional habits to build now.

flashcards.definition: A connected series of measured lines (courses), each with a length and a direction, used to locate points and compute their coordinates.
The Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors, the state agency that licenses and regulates surveyors (and engineers) under Tex. Occ. Code ch. 1071.
The Texas state agency that holds original land-grant records and issues patents; the source of Texas's original-survey, metes-and-bounds title system.

lessons.title: Week 1: Distance Measurement — Chaining & Tape Corrections
learning_objectives: Describe taping (chaining) procedure and the sources of error in measured distances.
Apply tape corrections for standardization (length), temperature, tension (pull), sag, and slope.
Compute a corrected horizontal distance from a measured (recorded) distance.
key_takeaways: Measured taped distances must be corrected for tape standard length, temperature, tension, sag, and slope before use.
Temperature correction = alpha * (T - T0) * L, with alpha = 0.00000645 per deg F for steel.
Slope-to-horizontal: H = sqrt(L^2 - h^2) (exact) or approximately H = L - h^2/(2L) for small slopes.
A correction that makes the tape effectively longer adds to the distance when laying out and subtracts when measuring — keep the sign rule straight.

content.html:
Measuring distance by tape (chaining)

Before EDM, distances were measured with a graduated steel tape (historically a chain, hence 'chaining'). Even today taping is essential for short ties, layout, and checks. The procedure stretches the tape between two points, holds it horizontal and under standard tension, and reads the distance. The art is in controlling the systematic errors that quietly bias every measurement: the tape is rarely exactly its nominal length, the temperature is rarely the standard temperature, the pull and the sag are rarely standard, and the ground is rarely level. Each of these produces a tape correction applied to the recorded distance.

Random errors (reading, marking, plumbing) are reduced by care and repetition; systematic errors are removed by computation. This lesson is about the systematic corrections.

Standardization (length), temperature, tension, sag

Standardization: a tape is compared against a known standard; if a 100.00 ft tape is actually 100.02 ft, every full tape length is 0.02 ft too long and a correction proportional to length applies. Temperature: steel expands and contracts; the correction is Ct = α(T − T0)L, where α ≈ 0.00000645 per °F for steel and T0 is the standardization temperature (usually 68°F). Tension (pull): applying more or less than standard pull stretches the tape elastically; the correction depends on the cross-sectional area and modulus of elasticity. Sag: a tape supported only at its ends hangs in a catenary and reads long (the chord is shorter than the tape), so the sag correction is always negative.

Slope correction

A tape stretched along sloping ground measures the slope distance L, but coordinate and area computations require the horizontal distance H. If the elevation difference between the tape ends is h, then exactly H = √(L² − h²). For gentle slopes the approximation H ≈ L − h²/(2L) is convenient and accurate. The slope correction (H − L) is always negative: the horizontal distance is shorter than the slope distance. If instead the slope is given by a vertical (zenith) angle, H = L·sin(zenith) or H = L·cos(vertical angle).

rows: + if tape too long, - if too short
+ if hotter than T0, - if colder

content.text: Sign rule of thumb: for MEASURING an unknown distance, if the tape is effectively too long the recorded distance is too SHORT, so add the correction. For LAYING OUT a known distance the rule reverses. State explicitly whether you are measuring or setting out.

Texas context: historic Texas field notes and GLO grants are recorded in VARAS (1 vara = 33 1/3 in = 2.7778 ft; 36 varas = exactly 100 ft). When retracing an old survey you may convert varas to feet, but never blindly apply a non-Texas vara value to a Texas grant.

content.alt: Steel tape stretched on a slope showing slope distance L, horizontal distance H, and elevation difference h
content.caption: Figure: Slope distance L reduced to horizontal distance H using elevation difference h.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Correct taped distances for standardization, temperature, tension, sag, and slope.
Temperature correction = alpha*(T - T0)*L; for steel alpha = 0.00000645 per deg F.
Sag and slope corrections are always negative; H = sqrt(L^2 - h^2).
1 vara = 2.7778 ft; 36 varas = 100 ft for Texas-record conversions.
Keep the measuring-vs-laying-out sign convention straight.

flashcards.definition: A systematic adjustment applied to a measured taped distance to account for standardization, temperature, tension, sag, or slope.
The change in a steel tape's length due to thermal expansion, C_t = alpha*(T - T0)*L, with alpha = 0.00000645 per deg F.
The always-negative correction for a tape hanging in a catenary between end supports, which makes the tape read longer than the true chord.
A Texas surveying unit equal to 33 1/3 inches (2.7778 ft); 36 varas equal exactly 100 feet and appear throughout GLO grant records.

lessons.title: Week 2: Compass Surveying, DD/DMS Conversions & Chaining Review
learning_objectives: Read and record bearings with a compass and distinguish bearings from azimuths.
Convert angles between decimal degrees (DD) and degrees-minutes-seconds (DMS).
Apply chaining corrections in combination with compass directions to lay out a short traverse.
key_takeaways: A compass reads a magnetic bearing as a quadrant direction (N/S, angle, E/W); azimuths run clockwise 0-360 from north.
DMS to DD: DD = deg + min/60 + sec/3600; reverse by extracting whole degrees, then minutes, then seconds.
Bearings are limited to 0-90 within a quadrant; azimuths span the full circle.
Compass surveying is low precision (magnetic, +/- ~15 minutes) and is superseded by total stations for boundary work.

content.html:
Compass surveying and bearings

A magnetic compass measures the horizontal direction of a line relative to magnetic north. Directions are recorded as bearings: a quadrant letter (N or S), an acute angle, and a second letter (E or W) — for example N 42°15' E. A bearing never exceeds 90° because it is measured from the nearer end of the meridian. The reverse direction of a line (its back bearing) keeps the same angle but swaps both letters: the back bearing of N 42°15' E is S 42°15' W.

Compass work is the historical root of land surveying and still appears in old deeds, but it is low precision (subject to local attraction and limited to roughly a quarter-degree). Modern boundary surveys carry direction with a total station, not a compass; compass surveying in this course builds intuition and lets you read historic descriptions.

Bearings vs. azimuths

An azimuth is a direction measured clockwise from a reference meridian (usually north), ranging continuously from 0° to 360°. Azimuths are convenient for computation because the latitude/departure formulas (Lat = d cos Az, Dep = d sin Az) automatically produce the correct signs in all four quadrants. Bearings are convenient for description and recording. You will convert constantly between them: in the NE quadrant azimuth = bearing angle; in the SE quadrant azimuth = 180° − bearing; in the SW, azimuth = 180° + bearing; in the NW, azimuth = 360° − bearing.

DD and DMS conversions

Surveyors record angles in degrees, minutes, and seconds (DMS) but compute in decimal degrees (DD). To convert DMS to DD, add the minutes divided by 60 and the seconds divided by 3600 to the whole degrees: 42°15'30" = 42 + 15/60 + 30/3600 = 42.2583°. To convert DD to DMS, take the whole degrees, multiply the fraction by 60 for whole minutes, then multiply that fraction by 60 for seconds: 42.2583° → 42°, 0.2583×60 = 15.5' → 15', 0.5×60 = 30" → 42°15'30". Carry enough decimal places; rounding a bearing too early can shift a long course by a meaningful distance.

content.text: Quick check: a bearing is never larger than 90 degrees. If a 'bearing' you computed exceeds 90, you forgot to fold it into the correct quadrant from its azimuth.

Texas context: magnetic bearings in old Texas deeds were referenced to the magnetic north of the survey date and must be corrected for declination and secular change before retracement. The footsteps of the original surveyor govern, not a recomputed modern bearing (Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Bearings: quadrant letter, acute angle (<=90), quadrant letter; back bearing swaps both letters.
Azimuths run clockwise 0-360 from north and give correct lat/dep signs automatically.
DMS to DD: DD = D + M/60 + S/3600; reverse by extracting degrees, minutes, then seconds.
Compass surveying is low precision and superseded by total stations for boundary work.
Old Texas magnetic bearings must be corrected for declination before retracement.

flashcards.definition: A direction expressed as a quadrant letter, an acute angle (0-90 deg) from the meridian, and a second letter, e.g. N 42 deg 15' E.
A direction measured clockwise from a reference meridian (usually north), ranging 0 to 360 degrees.
Converting degrees-minutes-seconds to decimal degrees by DD = D + M/60 + S/3600.
The bearing of a line viewed from the opposite end; the angle stays the same and both quadrant letters reverse.
flashcards.hint_1: Swap both N/S and E/W letters.

lessons.title: Week 3: Magnetic Declination Review, Smartphone Compass Apps, Field Notekeeping & Trade Magazines
learning_objectives: Define magnetic declination and convert between magnetic and true (geodetic) bearings.
Evaluate the accuracy and proper use of smartphone compass apps in survey reconnaissance.
Apply professional field-notekeeping standards and identify reputable surveying trade publications.
key_takeaways: Declination is the angle between magnetic north and true (geodetic) north; east declination is added, west is subtracted, when going from magnetic to true.
Declination changes with location and time (secular variation), so the date of an old survey matters for retracement.
Smartphone compass apps are reconnaissance aids only, not survey-grade instruments.
Complete, legible, contemporaneous field notes are a legal record; never erase, and reference the original surveyor's footsteps.

content.html:
Magnetic declination

Magnetic declination is the horizontal angle between magnetic north (where the compass points) and true (geodetic) north. It is east when magnetic north lies east of true north, and west otherwise. To convert a magnetic bearing to a true bearing, apply the declination: with east declination, add it; with west declination, subtract it (for bearings carefully tracked by quadrant). Declination varies by location and, crucially, by time — the so-called secular variation drifts the value by minutes per year. A bearing recorded magnetically in an 1870 GLO survey reflects that era's declination, which is why retracing old work requires the historic value, not today's.

content.label: East declination added, west subtracted (track quadrant)

content.html:
Smartphone compass apps

Modern phones contain a three-axis magnetometer that powers compass apps. These are genuinely useful for reconnaissance: orienting a site sketch, finding an approximate bearing to a distant monument, or confirming which way a line runs. But they are not survey instruments. Phone magnetometers are easily disturbed by nearby steel (vehicles, rebar, the truck you are leaning on), require frequent figure-eight calibration, and typically deliver only a few degrees of accuracy. Treat the app as a pocket reconnaissance compass; never record a boundary bearing from a phone.

Field notekeeping

Field notes are a permanent, legal record of what was observed. The professional standards are simple and strict: notes are made contemporaneously in the field, in ink, in a bound book or a controlled digital collector; they are legible and complete enough that an independent surveyor could reconstruct the work; nothing is erased — an error is struck through with a single line and corrected, preserving the original. Each page records the date, party, weather, instrument, and a sketch with north arrow, station names, observed angles, distances, and descriptions of monuments found or set. Good notes are the difference between a defensible survey and an unprovable one.

rows: Site orientation, rough azimuth to a monument

content.text: Professional development: reputable surveying trade publications (for example, The American Surveyor, POB - Point of Beginning, and xyHt) keep surveyors current on instruments, software, standards, and case law. Reading the trade press is part of the continuing-education habit TBPELS expects.

Texas context: because Texas titles trace to original GLO surveys often recorded with magnetic bearings, retracement requires correcting historic magnetic bearings for the declination of the survey date. The controlling rule remains to follow the footsteps of the original surveyor, not to substitute a modern recomputed bearing (Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Declination is the angle from magnetic north to true north; east is added, west subtracted (magnetic to true).
Declination varies by place and time (secular variation); historic survey dates matter for retracement.
Smartphone compass apps are reconnaissance aids, not survey instruments.
Field notes are a contemporaneous, ink, never-erased legal record; strike-through and correct errors.
Stay current through reputable surveying trade publications.

flashcards.definition: The horizontal angle between magnetic north and true (geodetic) north; east when magnetic north is east of true north, west otherwise.
flashcards.hint_1: Angle from magnetic north to true north.

flashcards.definition: The slow change in magnetic declination over time at a given location, important when retracing old magnetic-bearing surveys.
The contemporaneous, legible, never-erased written record of survey observations, made in ink or a controlled collector and admissible as evidence.

lessons.title: Week 4: Instrument Care, Tripod Best Practices & Maintenance Schedules
learning_objectives: Apply correct handling, transport, and storage practices for total stations and levels.
Set up a stable tripod and recognize how tripod faults propagate into measurement error.
Describe a maintenance and calibration schedule that keeps instruments within tolerance.
key_takeaways: Instruments are precision optics and electronics: lift by the standards, keep dry, transport in the case, and never leave on a tripod unattended.
A stable tripod with firmly seated, equal-tension legs and a clean tribrach is the foundation of every measurement.
Routine field checks (plate level, optical/laser plummet, collimation) catch drift before it corrupts data.
Follow a documented maintenance and periodic professional-calibration schedule; keep calibration records.

content.html:
Caring for a precision instrument

A total station is a precision optical-electronic instrument that can cost as much as a vehicle, and its accuracy depends on staying in adjustment. Handle it by the standards (the uprights), never by the telescope; remove it from the case by lifting straight up after loosening the clamps. Keep it dry — cover it in rain, and if it gets wet, dry it before casing to prevent fogging and corrosion. Never leave an instrument unattended on a tripod; wind, traffic, livestock, and curious passersby topple thousands of dollars in a second. Clean optics only with proper lens tissue, transport the instrument in its case (not on the tripod in a moving vehicle), and store it in a stable, dry environment.

Tripod best practices

The tripod is the literal foundation of every measurement, and tripod faults masquerade as measurement error. Spread the legs to a stable, roughly equal angle and press each firmly into the ground so it cannot settle or creep during the setup. Tighten the leg clamps snugly — a loose leg lets the instrument sag and drift mid-observation. Keep the tripod head approximately level before mounting the instrument so the fine leveling has range to work. Mount the instrument and immediately snug the centering screw. On hard surfaces, guard against the legs sliding; on soft ground, beware of slow settlement that opens an angle between backsight and foresight. A drifting setup is one of the most common sources of an unexplained traverse misclosure.

rows: Lift by the standards, not the telescope
Protects the telescope and axes from strain
Gives the leveling screws working range

content.text: Daily field checks: verify the plate (tubular/electronic) level, the optical or laser plummet, and that all clamps and tangent screws move smoothly. A two-minute check at the truck saves a wasted day of biased data.

content.html:
Maintenance and calibration schedules

Beyond daily care, instruments need a scheduled regimen. Daily/weekly: clean optics and housings, check battery health, confirm plate level and plummet, and run a quick collimation check. Periodically (and after any shock or temperature extreme): perform field adjustments for line-of-sight (horizontal) collimation, vertical-index error, and tilt sensor. Annually or per manufacturer interval: send the instrument to an authorized service center for full calibration against traceable standards, and keep the calibration certificate. EDM accuracy should be verified on a known baseline. A documented schedule and retained records demonstrate that the data behind a survey was produced by an instrument in tolerance — useful if the work is ever challenged.

content.text: Texas context: TBPELS standards of practice (22 TAC ch. 138) require that surveys meet stated accuracy standards. Using an out-of-adjustment instrument, or failing to document calibration, can undermine the defensibility of a survey if it is challenged.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Handle instruments by the standards, keep them dry, transport in the case, never leave unattended on a tripod.
A firm, level, equal-tension tripod with snug clamps prevents settlement and drift.
Run daily field checks (level, plummet, collimation) to catch drift early.
Follow daily/periodic/annual maintenance and professional-calibration schedules; keep records.
Documented in-tolerance instruments support a defensible survey under TBPELS standards.

flashcards.definition: The two upright supports that carry the telescope's horizontal axis; an instrument should be lifted by the standards, never the telescope.
A field procedure that detects line-of-sight (and vertical-index) error by observing on both faces; corrected so the line of sight is truly perpendicular to the axes.
flashcards.hint_1: Both-face check of line of sight.

flashcards.definition: Documentation that an instrument was checked against traceable standards on a schedule, supporting the accuracy and defensibility of survey data.
flashcards.hint_1: Proof the instrument was in tolerance.

lessons.title: Week 5: Instrument Leveling & Setup Over a Point
learning_objectives: Set up and level a total station precisely over a ground mark using the plummet and leveling screws.
Distinguish coarse leveling (tripod legs/bubble) from fine leveling (leveling screws) and centering.
Explain how setup (centering) error and the height of instrument affect measured angles and reductions.
key_takeaways: Setup is an iterative loop: rough-center with the plummet, coarse-level by adjusting tripod legs, fine-level with the leveling screws, then re-center by sliding the tribrach.
Fine leveling: align the plate bubble parallel to two screws and center it, rotate 90 degrees, center with the third screw, then check all around.
Centering (setup) error is most damaging on short sights and produces angular error.
Record the height of instrument (HI) carefully; it feeds trigonometric leveling and 3D reductions.

content.html:
The setup loop

Setting up a total station precisely over a point is a learned, iterative skill. The sequence is: (1) set the tripod roughly centered and level over the mark, legs firm; (2) rough-center using the optical or laser plummet, adjusting tripod-leg lengths to bring the bubble of the circular level approximately in while keeping the plummet near the point; (3) coarse-level with the circular (bull's-eye) bubble by leg adjustment; (4) fine-level with the leveling screws against the plate level; (5) re-center precisely by loosening the tribrach and sliding the instrument on the tripod head until the plummet is exactly on the mark. Because leveling and centering interact, you iterate steps 4 and 5 until both are satisfied simultaneously.

Fine leveling with the screws

Fine leveling follows a fixed routine that exploits the three leveling screws. First, rotate the alidade until the plate level is parallel to a line through two of the screws; turn those two screws in opposite directions to center the bubble (the bubble follows your left thumb). Second, rotate the instrument 90° so the level lies over the third screw; use only that screw to center the bubble. Third, return and check; repeat until the bubble stays centered in all directions. With an electronic level, the display guides the same two-then-one screw motions. A correctly leveled instrument has its vertical axis truly vertical, which is what makes horizontal angles meaningful.

content.label: Angular error from a centering error e_c at sight distance D

content.html:
Why setup precision matters

A small centering error ec (the instrument off the mark) introduces an angular error that grows as the sight distance D shrinks: the angular error in radians is roughly ec/D, or ec/D × 206265 in arc-seconds. Miscentering by 0.005 ft (about 1.5 mm) on a 100-ft sight is about 10 arc-seconds of angular error; on a 20-ft sight it is five times worse. This is why short sights and careless centering are so damaging, and why the field-procedure lesson stressed balanced, not-too-short courses. Finally, record the height of instrument (HI) — the vertical distance from the ground mark to the instrument's trunnion axis — because it feeds trigonometric leveling and 3D coordinate reductions.

content.text: Two-then-one rule: with the plate level over two screws, turn both (opposite directions) to center; rotate 90 degrees and use only the third screw. The bubble moves toward your left thumb.

content.alt: Total station on a tripod over a ground monument, showing the leveling screws, plate bubble, and optical plummet line to the point
content.caption: Figure: Leveling and centering a total station over a point.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Setup iterates: rough-center, coarse-level, fine-level with screws, then slide to center exactly.
Fine leveling: two screws (opposite) to center, rotate 90 deg, third screw, then recheck.
Centering error of e_c at sight D gives angular error ~ e_c/D (rad); worst on short sights.
A truly vertical axis is what makes horizontal angles valid.
Record the height of instrument (HI) for trigonometric leveling and 3D reductions.

flashcards.definition: The downward-pointing sight or laser used to center the instrument's vertical axis exactly over a ground mark.
The sensitive tubular or electronic level used with the three leveling screws to make the instrument's vertical axis truly vertical.
The horizontal offset of the instrument from the true mark, which produces an angular error approximately equal to the offset divided by the sight distance.
flashcards.hint_1: Off-mark error; worst on short sights.

flashcards.definition: The vertical distance from the occupied ground mark up to the instrument's trunnion axis, used in trigonometric leveling and 3D reductions.

lessons.title: Week 6: Types of Angles & Angle Measurement
learning_objectives: Distinguish interior, exterior, deflection, and angle-to-the-right measurements.
Compute azimuths and bearings of successive courses from measured angles.
Apply face-left/face-right observation and angle repetition to control instrumental error.
key_takeaways: Interior angles sum to (n-2)*180 for a closed polygon; deflection angles sum to 360; exterior angles sum to (n+2)*180.
Angle to the right (clockwise from backsight to foresight) is the modern total-station convention.
Forward azimuth of next course = back azimuth of current course + angle to the right (mod 360).
Observing face-left and face-right (D&R) and repeating angles cancels collimation and axis errors.

content.html:
Kinds of angles

Surveyors measure horizontal angles in several conventions. An interior angle is measured inside a closed polygon at each station; for an n-sided closed traverse the interior angles must sum to (n − 2) × 180°. An exterior angle is its supplement outside the polygon; exterior angles sum to (n + 2) × 180°. A deflection angle is measured right (R) or left (L) from the prolongation of the preceding course — favored on route surveys; for a closed loop the deflection angles algebraically sum to 360°. An angle to the right is the clockwise angle from the backsight to the foresight and is the dominant convention for modern total-station traverses because it carries direction unambiguously.

Carrying azimuth through a traverse

Once you observe angles to the right, you can propagate azimuths around the traverse. The rule is: the forward azimuth of the next course equals the back azimuth of the current course plus the measured angle to the right, reduced modulo 360°. The back azimuth is the forward azimuth ± 180°. Starting from one known azimuth, you can compute the azimuth of every course, and from each azimuth the corresponding bearing. This computation is the bridge from raw angle observations to the directions needed for latitudes and departures.

content.label: Azimuth propagation using angles to the right

content.html:
Controlling instrumental error

No instrument is perfect, so angles are observed to cancel systematic errors. Observing on both telescope faces — face-left (direct) and face-right (reversed), abbreviated D&R — and averaging eliminates line-of-sight (collimation) error and horizontal-axis (trunnion) error, because those errors flip sign between faces. Repeating the angle several times and averaging reduces random pointing and reading error. Closing the horizon (turning all the way around the point so the angles total 360°) catches blunders on station. Together these habits turn a good instrument into trustworthy data and are expected on any boundary or control traverse.

content.text: Angular closure check: for a closed loop, compare the sum of measured interior angles with (n-2)*180. Distribute the small angular misclosure equally among the angles BEFORE computing azimuths and lat/dep.

Texas context: TBPELS standards (22 TAC ch. 138) expect angular observations adequate to meet the survey's accuracy class. For boundary work, document repetition and both-face observation; a single un-repeated angle is poor practice if challenged.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Interior angles sum to (n-2)*180; exterior to (n+2)*180; deflection angles to 360 deg.
Angle to the right (clockwise BS to FS) is the modern convention.
Az_next = (Az_prev +/- 180 + angle right) mod 360.
Face-left/face-right (D&R) cancels collimation and trunnion-axis errors; repetition reduces random error.
Check angular closure and distribute the small misclosure before computing azimuths.

flashcards.definition: An angle measured inside a closed polygon at a station; the interior angles of an n-sided closed traverse sum to (n-2)*180 degrees.
The angle turned right or left from the prolongation of the preceding course; deflection angles of a closed loop algebraically sum to 360 degrees.
The clockwise angle measured from the backsight to the foresight, the standard convention for modern total-station traverses.
The difference between the measured sum of interior angles and (n-2)*180 degrees, distributed among the angles before computing azimuths.

lessons.title: Week 7: Transit Rule and Other Adjustment Methods
learning_objectives: Compute latitudes and departures and the misclosure of a closed traverse.
Apply the compass (Bowditch) and transit rules to distribute misclosure, and contrast their assumptions.
Recognize least-squares adjustment as the rigorous modern alternative.
key_takeaways: Lat = d*cos(bearing), Dep = d*sin(bearing); for a closed loop the sums should be zero, and the residuals are the misclosures.
Compass (Bowditch) rule: correction proportional to course length; transit rule: proportional to component magnitude (|Lat|, |Dep|).
Transit rule assumes angles are more precise than distances and disturbs bearings less than the compass rule.
Least squares rigorously weights every observation by its precision and is the modern professional standard.

content.html:
From courses to misclosure

To adjust a traverse you first resolve each course into a latitude (north-south component, Lat = d cos α) and a departure (east-west component, Dep = d sin α). For a closed-loop traverse the latitudes should sum to zero and the departures should sum to zero. The small residual sums are the misclosure in latitude (EL = ΣLat) and the misclosure in departure (ED = ΣDep). Their hypotenuse, E = hypot(EL, ED), is the linear misclosure, and perimeter ÷ E gives the ratio of closure 1:N. Adjustment distributes EL and ED back into the courses so the traverse closes — but only after the ratio of closure passes and any blunder has been hunted out.

Compass (Bowditch) rule

The compass or Bowditch rule is the workhorse adjustment. It assumes the precision of angles and distances is comparable, so accumulated error is proportional to course length. The correction to a course's latitude is −EL×(di/P) and to its departure is −ED×(di/P), where P is the perimeter. Longer courses receive proportionally larger corrections. After applying them, the adjusted latitudes and departures each sum to zero. The compass rule fits ordinary total-station boundary traverses, where angles and EDM distances are observed to similar relative precision, and is the default in most software.

Transit rule

The transit rule rests on a different assumption: the angles are measured more precisely than the distances. Because distance error dominates, the misclosure is distributed in proportion to the magnitude of each course's latitude and departure rather than its length: CLat,i = −EL×(|Lati|/Σ|Lat|) and CDep,i = −ED×(|Depi|/Σ|Dep|). The two components are weighted independently. A practical signature is that the transit rule disturbs the computed bearings less than the compass rule (it holds directions, pushes lengths). Its weakness is erratic behavior when a course's latitude or departure is near zero (a nearly E-W or N-S line), so modern practice rarely prefers it.

rows: Each observation weighted by its precision

content.text: Other methods: the Crandall rule (holds angles fixed, adjusts distances by least squares) and the rigorous least-squares adjustment generalize these ideas. Least squares also yields error ellipses that describe the positional uncertainty of each station.

Texas context: Always confirm the ratio of closure meets the required standard for the survey class (TBPELS 22 TAC ch. 138 and the TSPS Manual of Practice) BEFORE adjusting. Spreading an undetected blunder across all courses corrupts otherwise-good boundary data.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Compute Lat = d cos(bearing), Dep = d sin(bearing); misclosure E = hypot(sum Lat, sum Dep).
Compass rule weights corrections by course length (d_i/P).
Transit rule weights by component magnitude (|Lat|, |Dep|) and disturbs bearings less.
The transit rule misbehaves when a component is near zero; least squares is the rigorous modern choice.
Verify the ratio of closure and hunt blunders BEFORE adjusting.

flashcards.definition: The north-south component (Lat = d cos bearing) and east-west component (Dep = d sin bearing) of a course.
flashcards.hint_1: N-S = d cos; E-W = d sin.

flashcards.definition: An adjustment distributing latitude and departure misclosure in proportion to course length, assuming comparable angle and distance precision.
An adjustment distributing misclosure in proportion to each course's |latitude| and |departure|, assuming angles are more precise than distances.
A rigorous adjustment that weights every observation by its estimated precision, producing the statistically best coordinates and error ellipses.

lessons.title: Week 8: Midterm Exam
learning_objectives: Integrate distance, direction, angle, and adjustment skills from Weeks 1-7.
Execute a complete short-traverse computation: lat/dep, misclosure, ratio of closure, and adjustment.
Self-assess readiness using a structured review of the first-half learning objectives.
key_takeaways: The midterm covers Weeks 1-7: taping corrections, bearings/azimuths and DMS, declination, instrument care/setup, angle types, latitudes/departures, misclosure, and the compass and transit rules.
Be fluent in the full short-traverse workflow: components -> misclosure -> ratio -> adjust.
Show every step with units and a check; partial credit follows disciplined work.
Review by re-working problems, not just re-reading notes.

content.html:
What the midterm covers

The midterm assesses the first half of SRVY 1341, Weeks 1-7. Expect a balance of conceptual questions and computations drawn from: distance measurement and tape corrections (standardization, temperature, sag, slope); compass surveying, bearings vs. azimuths, and DD/DMS conversions; magnetic declination and field-note standards; instrument care, setup, and leveling; types of angles and azimuth propagation; and the heart of the half — latitudes and departures, misclosure, ratio of closure, and the compass (Bowditch) and transit adjustment rules.

The computation you must own

The single most important skill is the short-traverse workflow. Given a few courses (bearings and horizontal distances) you should be able to, without hesitation: (1) resolve each into latitude and departure with correct signs; (2) sum to get EL and ED; (3) compute the linear misclosure E = hypot(EL, ED); (4) report the ratio of closure 1:N = perimeter/E; and (5) apply the compass or transit corrections and confirm the adjusted sums close to zero. Practice until each step is automatic and you can carry the units and a self-check through cleanly.

rows: Correct a taped distance; reduce slope to horizontal
Convert magnetic to true; keep proper field notes
Care, setup, level, and center over a point

content.text: Exam strategy: write the governing formula first, substitute with units, then evaluate. A labeled, step-by-step solution earns partial credit even if a single arithmetic slip occurs.

Texas context: licensure exams (the Fundamentals/SIT and the Texas RPLS exams administered under TBPELS) reward exactly this disciplined traverse workflow. Building the habit now pays off at the board exam.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Midterm spans Weeks 1-7: measurement, direction, angles, instruments, and adjustment.
Own the short-traverse workflow: lat/dep -> misclosure -> ratio -> adjust.
Write the formula, substitute with units, evaluate, and check.
Review by re-working problems; partial credit rewards clear steps.

flashcards.definition: The exam-critical sequence: resolve courses into lat/dep, sum for misclosure, compute ratio of closure, then adjust by compass or transit rule.
Perimeter divided by linear misclosure, reported as 1:N with N = round(perimeter / misclosure); larger N is better.

lessons.title: Week 9: Coordinate Geometry — Inversing and Intersections
learning_objectives: Compute the distance and azimuth between two coordinated points (the inverse).
Solve bearing-bearing and bearing-distance intersection problems.
Apply coordinate geometry (COGO) to locate points and check field work.
key_takeaways: Inverse: distance = hypot(dE, dN); azimuth = atan2(dE, dN) reduced to 0-360.
atan2(dE, dN) (note the order) returns the azimuth measured clockwise from north.
Bearing-bearing intersection finds where two lines of known direction from two known points cross.
COGO turns measured/computed coordinates into distances, directions, and intersection points for layout and checks.

content.html:
The inverse: distance and direction from coordinates

Coordinate geometry (COGO) works the traverse problem in reverse. Given two points with coordinates (N1, E1) and (N2, E2), the inverse computes the distance and azimuth between them. The differences are ΔN = N2 − N1 (the latitude) and ΔE = E2 − E1 (the departure). The distance is the hypotenuse, distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN), and the azimuth is atan2(ΔE, ΔN) — note the departure-over-latitude order, because azimuth is measured clockwise from north. Reduce a negative result by adding 360°. The inverse is the everyday tool for computing a course between any two known points: corner to corner, control to corner, or instrument to a stake-out target.

content.label: Inverse: distance and azimuth between two points

content.html:
Intersections

An intersection finds a new point from two lines of constraint. In a bearing-bearing intersection, two known points each emit a line of known direction (azimuth); the new point is where the lines cross — solved by writing each line parametrically and equating, or by the law of sines on the triangle the two stations form. In a bearing-distance (direction-distance) intersection, you know a direction from one point and a distance from another (a line meets a circle, giving up to two solutions). In a distance-distance intersection, two known distances from two known points locate the new point (two circles, up to two intersections) — the basis of trilateration. Intersections are how surveyors compute corner positions, set out points from multiple ties, and resolve geometry that was not directly measured.

Using COGO to check and lay out

COGO is both a computation and a checking tool. After adjusting a traverse you can inverse between any two stations and compare the computed course against the field-measured one; a discrepancy flags a blunder. For layout (stake-out), you inverse from the occupied instrument point to the design point to get the azimuth and distance to turn and measure. Modern data collectors run these COGO routines continuously, but you must understand the underlying trigonometry to set them up correctly, recognize a wrong answer, and defend the geometry. The inverse and the intersection are the two COGO operations you will use most.

content.text: Watch the atan2 argument order: azimuth from north uses atan2(departure, latitude) = atan2(dE, dN). Swapping the arguments gives the angle from the east axis instead — a classic COGO mistake.

Texas context: COGO coordinates for boundary work are normally carried on the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83). Remember that grid azimuths and distances differ from geodetic/ground values; track whether you are working in grid or ground (TBPELS 22 TAC ch. 138).

content.alt: Two known points with rays of known azimuth crossing at a computed intersection point
content.caption: Figure: Bearing-bearing intersection of two known points.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Inverse: distance = hypot(dE, dN); azimuth = atan2(dE, dN), reduced to 0-360.
Use atan2(departure, latitude) order so the azimuth is from north.
Bearing-bearing gives one intersection; bearing-distance and distance-distance give up to two.
Inverse between adjusted stations to check field work; inverse from instrument to design point to stake out.
Track grid vs. ground when working on the Texas State Plane Coordinate System.

flashcards.definition: Computing the distance and azimuth between two coordinated points: distance = hypot(dE, dN), azimuth = atan2(dE, dN).
The two-argument arctangent that returns an azimuth from north as atan2(departure, latitude) = atan2(dE, dN), quadrant-correct over 0-360.
Locating a point as the crossing of two lines of known direction emanating from two known points.
flashcards.hint_1: Two directions cross at a point.

flashcards.definition: Locating a point from two known distances to two known points; geometrically the intersection of two circles.

lessons.title: Week 10: Area Computation — Coordinate and DMD Methods
learning_objectives: Compute a parcel area from balanced coordinates by the coordinate (shoelace) method.
Compute area by the double-meridian-distance (DMD) method and show its equivalence.
Convert areas between square feet, acres, and Texas units, and apply the balance-first rule.
key_takeaways: Coordinate (shoelace) area: A = (1/2)|sum(x_i*y_{i+1} - x_{i+1}*y_i)| on a balanced traverse.
DMD method: area = (1/2)|sum(DMD_i * Lat_i)|, where DMD accumulates departures; equivalent to the coordinate method.
Always balance (adjust) the traverse first; an unclosed figure gives a wrong area.
1 acre = 43,560 sq ft; in Texas records 1 acre ~ 5,645.4 sq varas and 1 labor ~ 177.1 acres.

content.html:
Area by coordinates (shoelace)

Once a traverse is balanced and every corner has coordinates, the parcel area follows directly from the coordinate (shoelace) method. Taking the corners in order around the perimeter (consistently clockwise or counterclockwise), the area is half the absolute value of the sum of cross-products xiyi+1 − xi+1yi, with indices cycling so the last corner closes back to the first. The method is exact for any simple closed polygon and is the modern computation of choice. The balance-first rule is essential: an unadjusted figure does not close, and its 'area' silently includes the misclosure gap.

Double meridian distance (DMD)

The double-meridian-distance (DMD) method is the classical hand-tabulation that predates coordinates and gives the same answer. The meridian distance of a course is the perpendicular distance from a reference meridian to the course's midpoint; doubling it avoids fractions. The DMD of the first course equals its own departure; each subsequent DMD equals the previous DMD plus the previous departure plus the current departure. The area is then A = ½|Σ(DMDi × Lati)|. DMD is worth knowing because old records, exams, and some software trace to it, and because it reinforces how latitudes and departures encode area.

Units and conversions

Computed areas come out in square feet (when coordinates are in feet) and are reported in acres for land: divide square feet by 43,560. A legal description typically states the area as 'containing X acres, more or less.' In Texas you may also meet historic units: an acre is about 5,645.4 square varas, a labor is 1,000,000 square varas (≈ 177.1 acres), and a league (sitio) is 25,000,000 square varas (≈ 4,428.4 acres). When an old grant gives acreage in labors or leagues, convert with care, remembering historic varas varied slightly.

content.text: The two methods are identical mathematics, just bookkeeping differences. Use the coordinate method when you already have coordinates; use DMD when working straight from a balanced lat/dep table by hand.

Texas context: report area consistent with the controlling description and the original grant. Quantity/area is the LOWEST-dignity call in Texas boundary law (after natural and artificial monuments, adjoinder, course, and distance), so a computed acreage never overrides a found monument (Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257).

content.alt: Closed polygon on a coordinate grid with corner coordinates labeled, illustrating the shoelace cross-products
content.caption: Figure: Area by the coordinate (shoelace) method on a balanced traverse.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Coordinate (shoelace) area: A = (1/2)|sum(x_i*y_{i+1} - x_{i+1}*y_i)| on balanced coordinates.
DMD area: A = (1/2)|sum(DMD_i * Lat_i)|; DMD accumulates departures; same result.
Balance the traverse first; an unclosed figure gives a wrong area.
acres = sq ft / 43,560; in Texas 1 acre ~ 5,645.4 sq varas, 1 labor ~ 177.1 acres.
Area is the lowest-dignity call and never overrides a found monument.

flashcards.definition: Area of a closed polygon as half the absolute value of the sum of cross-products x_i*y_{i+1} - x_{i+1}*y_i, using balanced coordinates in perimeter order.
A classical area method where DMD_i = DMD_{i-1} + Dep_{i-1} + Dep_i and area = (1/2)|sum(DMD_i * Lat_i)|.
A unit of land area equal to 43,560 square feet (about 5,645.4 square varas in Texas).
A Texas land-area unit of 1,000,000 square varas, approximately 177.1 acres.

lessons.title: Week 11: Boundary Determination I — Evidence and Monuments
learning_objectives: Distinguish natural and artificial monuments and rank evidence by dignity of calls.
Explain the controlling role of found original monuments over recomputed measurements.
Apply the Texas dignity-of-calls hierarchy and intent in evaluating boundary evidence.
key_takeaways: Boundary location is an evidence problem: monuments, records, and measurements are weighed, not just recomputed.
Dignity of calls (Texas): natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity/area.
A found, identified original monument controls over conflicting course-and-distance calls.
All calls yield to the intent of the parties and the footsteps of the original surveyor (Stafford v. King).

content.html:
Boundaries are an evidence problem

Determining a boundary is not the same as computing a traverse. The traverse gives precise measurements; the boundary is where the law — through the original grant, the monuments, and the evidence on the ground — says the line actually is. The surveyor's task in retracement is to gather and weigh evidence: original and later monuments, recorded descriptions, occupation, and measurements, then locate the line consistent with the controlling intent. Precise measurement of the wrong point is worthless; the highest skill is reading the evidence correctly.

Monuments: natural and artificial

A monument is a physical object that marks a corner or line. Natural monuments are features that exist independent of the survey — rivers, creeks, lakes, prominent rocks, ridges — and are the most reliable evidence because they are large, durable, and hard to mistake. Artificial monuments are objects placed by people to mark the survey — set iron rods, pipes, marked (bearing) trees, stone mounds, fences called for in the description. A monument that can be identified as original (the very object the original surveyor set or called for) is powerful evidence: it shows where the line was actually run, which is the controlling question.

Dignity of calls

When the calls in a description conflict, they are ranked by reliability, the dignity of calls, highest to lowest: (1) natural monuments; (2) artificial monuments; (3) adjoinder (an adjacent, already-established line); (4) course (direction); (5) distance; (6) quantity/area. This is the foundational construction rule from Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867), which collapsed it to 'natural objects, artificial objects, then course and distance,' later refined by treatises. The hierarchy is a set of rebuttable presumptions: a higher-dignity call ordinarily controls a lower one, so a found original monument controls over a conflicting bearing or distance.

rows: Iron rod, pipe, bearing tree, mound, called fence

content.text: Original vs. obliterated vs. lost: an ORIGINAL monument still in place controls; an OBLITERATED corner can be recovered from collateral evidence (witnesses, fences, ties) and still controls; only a truly LOST corner, unrecoverable by any evidence, is re-set by proportioning.

Texas context: the dignity-of-calls hierarchy is only a rule of construction. It always yields to (i) the intent of the parties on the face of the grant and (ii) the footsteps of the original surveyor, located where the lines were actually run (Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257). A lower-dignity call can prevail when the instrument and circumstances show that was the intent.

content.alt: Sketch ranking a creek (natural), an iron rod (artificial), an adjoiner line, and course/distance calls by dignity
content.caption: Figure: Dignity of calls, from natural monuments down to quantity.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Boundary location weighs evidence: monuments, records, occupation, and measurements.
Dignity of calls: natural > artificial > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity/area.
A found, identified original monument controls over conflicting course-and-distance calls.
Original monuments control; obliterated corners are recovered from collateral evidence; only truly lost corners are proportioned.
All calls yield to intent and the original surveyor's footsteps (Stafford v. King).

flashcards.definition: A physical object that marks a corner or line; natural monuments (rivers, rocks) outrank artificial ones (iron rods, bearing trees).
The reliability hierarchy for conflicting description calls: natural monument > artificial monument > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity.
The foundational 1867 Texas case (30 Tex. 257) establishing the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the footsteps-of-the-original-surveyor doctrine.
An obliterated corner can be recovered from collateral evidence and still controls; only a truly lost corner, unrecoverable by evidence, is re-set by proportioning.
flashcards.hint_1: Obliterated is recoverable; lost is proportioned.

lessons.title: Week 12: Boundary Determination II — Retracement Principles
learning_objectives: Apply the footsteps doctrine: a resurvey retraces, it does not relocate, the original boundary.
Apply senior/junior rights and proportionate measurement to sequential and simultaneous conveyances.
Recognize the role of occupation, acquiescence, and the surveyor's duty in retracement.
key_takeaways: A resurvey re-establishes the ORIGINAL lines and corners; the original surveyor's footsteps control over inconsistent field-note calls.
Sequential conveyances follow senior/junior rights: the senior takes its full description, the junior absorbs excess or deficiency.
Simultaneous (platted) lots are of equal dignity: excess/deficiency is apportioned proportionately, never absorbed by one lot.
Proportionate measurement is a last resort, used only for genuinely lost corners after monuments and collateral evidence fail.

content.html:
Retracement is following footsteps

A resurvey is a retracement, not a fresh survey. It does not create or relocate boundaries; it re-establishes the lines and corners of the original survey. The cardinal Texas rule is to follow the footsteps of the original surveyor: locate the lines where they were actually run, as shown by the monuments and best evidence, even when later, more precise measurements disagree with the original field-note calls. The retracing surveyor's job is to find what the original surveyor did, not to improve on it. This doctrine runs straight from Stafford v. King through modern Texas cases.

Senior and junior rights

When a grantor conveys parcels sequentially (one after another over time), the earlier senior conveyance takes the full measure of its description; the later junior conveyance takes only the remainder. A grantor cannot convey land already conveyed to a senior grantee, so any overlap or gap is resolved in the senior's favor and the junior absorbs the excess or deficiency. Seniority does not enlarge a grant beyond its own description — it only governs conflicts. The Texas GLO patent system fits this naturally: the original patent is senior to later subdivisions carved from it.

Simultaneous conveyances and proportioning

By contrast, lots created simultaneously — classically, all lots on a recorded subdivision plat — are of equal dignity; no lot is senior. When the measured distance between two controlling, undisputed monuments differs from the record (platted) distance, the excess or deficiency is apportioned proportionately among the interior lots in proportion to their record lengths; no single lot absorbs the whole error. But apportionment is a last resort: found original monuments and recoverable (obliterated) corners control first. Proportioning applies only where a corner is genuinely lost, and only to ordinary distributed error — an isolable gross blunder is placed where it belongs.

rows: Senior/junior: senior whole, junior takes remainder

content.text: Occupation and acquiescence: long-standing occupation lines (fences, use) are evidence of where the original line was, and Texas applies agreed-boundary and acquiescence doctrines narrowly. They inform, but do not automatically override, the record and monuments.

Texas context: there is NO controlling Texas Supreme Court case adopting 'single/double proportionate measurement' by name; in Texas, proportioning is established surveying practice used sparingly, and original-survey footsteps dominate because Texas land descends from GLO original surveys, not a federal PLSS grid. Honor junior/senior rights and the record chain (TBPELS 22 TAC ch. 138).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A resurvey retraces; the original surveyor's footsteps control over inconsistent field-note calls.
Sequential conveyances: senior takes its full description, junior absorbs excess/deficiency.
Simultaneous (platted) lots: apportion excess/deficiency proportionately; none is senior.
Proportioning is a last resort for genuinely lost corners only.
In Texas, footsteps and the GLO original survey dominate; proportioning is used sparingly.

flashcards.definition: The Texas retracement rule that a resurvey re-establishes the original survey, following where the original surveyor actually ran the lines even against later measurements.
When parcels are conveyed sequentially, the senior conveyance takes its full description and the junior takes only the remainder, absorbing excess or deficiency.
Distributing excess or deficiency among simultaneously created lots in proportion to their record lengths; a last resort used only for genuinely lost corners.
flashcards.hint_1: Distribute error by record length; last resort.

flashcards.definition: Simultaneous (platted) lots share equal dignity and are apportioned; sequential carve-outs follow senior/junior rights.

lessons.title: Week 13: Subdivision Concepts and Curve Data
learning_objectives: Describe subdivision platting concepts: blocks, lots, rights-of-way, and easements.
Compute the principal elements of a simple horizontal circular curve from radius and central angle.
Relate curve data (R, delta, T, L, C) used on plats and in roadway alignments.
key_takeaways: A subdivision plat creates lots simultaneously, with blocks, lot lines, streets/rights-of-way, and easements dedicated as shown.
Curve tangent: T = R*tan(delta/2); curve length: L = R*toRad(delta) = R*delta*PI/180.
Long chord: C = 2*R*sin(delta/2); the degree of curve relates R to a standard arc or chord.
Subdivision lines on curved frontage are described by curve data (radius, delta, arc length, chord bearing/length).

content.html:
Subdivision concepts

A subdivision divides a parent tract into lots for sale or development, recorded on a plat. The plat organizes land into blocks (groups of lots) separated by streets / rights-of-way, and dedicates easements (utility, drainage, access) as shown. Because all lots are created by the single recorded instrument, they are a simultaneous conveyance of equal dignity — the seniority and apportionment rules from retracement apply directly. Lot lines are described relative to the block and the platted monuments; the plat's recorded dimensions, bearings, and curve data govern, subject always to found monuments controlling over measurement.

Horizontal circular curves

Street and roadway alignments turn with horizontal circular curves, and lots fronting them have curved boundaries described by curve data. A simple circular curve is defined by its radius R and its central (deflection) angle Δ — the angle the alignment turns through. From these two values every other element follows. The point of curvature (PC) is where the tangent meets the curve; the point of tangency (PT) is where the curve rejoins the tangent; the point of intersection (PI) is where the two tangents would meet.

content.label: Curve tangent T, arc length L, and long chord C

content.html:
The curve elements

The principal elements are: the tangent distance T = R·tan(Δ/2), the distance from PC (or PT) back to the PI; the curve (arc) length L = R·Δ in radians = R·Δ·π/180, the length measured along the arc; the long chord C = 2R·sin(Δ/2), the straight-line distance from PC to PT; the external distance E and middle ordinate M, which describe how far the curve stands from the PI and the chord. The degree of curve (arc or chord definition) is the central angle subtending a standard 100-ft arc or chord and is an alternative way to specify sharpness. A plat note for curved frontage typically lists R, Δ, L, and the chord bearing and length.

content.text: Degrees vs. radians: arc length needs the central angle in RADIANS (multiply degrees by PI/180), while T and C use the half-angle in the trig functions. Mixing the two is the most common curve-computation error.

Texas context: subdivision platting in Texas is governed by Tex. Local Government Code ch. 212 (municipal) and ch. 232 (county). Plats must meet local and state platting requirements before recording, and a recorded plat creates the lots simultaneously, fixing equal-dignity lot lines and dedicated rights-of-way and easements.

content.alt: Simple horizontal curve showing PC, PT, PI, radius R, central angle delta, tangent T, arc L, and long chord C
content.caption: Figure: Elements of a simple horizontal circular curve.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A subdivision plat creates equal-dignity lots simultaneously, with blocks, rights-of-way, and dedicated easements.
Tangent T = R*tan(delta/2); arc length L = R*delta*PI/180; long chord C = 2*R*sin(delta/2).
Arc length needs delta in radians; T and C use the half-angle.
Curved lot frontage is described by R, delta, arc length, and chord bearing/length.
Texas platting follows Local Government Code ch. 212/232.

flashcards.definition: A recorded instrument dividing a parent tract into blocks and lots, dedicating streets/rights-of-way and easements, and creating all lots simultaneously.
flashcards.hint_1: Recorded division into lots and ROW.

flashcards.definition: The distance from the point of curvature (or tangency) to the point of intersection of a horizontal curve, T = R*tan(delta/2).
The length measured along a circular curve's arc, L = R*delta with delta in radians = R*delta*PI/180.
The straight-line distance from the point of curvature to the point of tangency of a circular curve, C = 2*R*sin(delta/2).

lessons.title: Week 14: Computer Applications in Traverse Adjustment
learning_objectives: Use COGO and survey software to compute, adjust, and report a traverse.
Contrast spreadsheet/compass-rule workflows with rigorous least-squares software.
Apply verification habits so software output is checked, not blindly trusted.
key_takeaways: Survey software automates lat/dep, misclosure, ratio of closure, adjustment, coordinates, and area, but requires correct input and units.
Least-squares software weights observations by precision and reports error ellipses and statistics.
Always verify software output with independent checks (inverse, closure, sanity of magnitudes).
Document the software, settings, datum, and grid/ground basis used.

content.html:
Software in the traverse workflow

Every step you have computed by hand is automated in survey software and data collectors: resolving courses into latitudes and departures, summing the misclosure, reporting the ratio of closure, applying an adjustment (compass, transit, Crandall, or least squares), accumulating coordinates, and computing area. Packages such as spreadsheets for the compass rule, and dedicated COGO/least-squares programs, turn hours of tabulation into seconds. The catch is unchanged: software computes exactly what you tell it, so correct input — right bearings, horizontal distances, consistent units, the proper datum and zone — is everything.

Compass-rule spreadsheets vs. least squares

Two tiers of tools coexist. A compass-rule spreadsheet mirrors the hand method: enter bearings and distances, and it returns lat/dep, misclosure, ratio, corrections, coordinates, and area. It is transparent and fine for ordinary closed-loop boundary traverses. Least-squares software is more rigorous: it accepts redundant observations (angles, distances, GNSS vectors), weights each by its estimated precision, and produces the statistically best coordinates along with error ellipses and statistics (standard deviations, residuals, a chi-square test). For networks, control, and any work mixing measurement types, least squares is the professional standard; the compass rule remains the everyday tool for a simple loop.

content.text: Garbage in, garbage out: software cannot detect a transposed digit, a slope distance entered as horizontal, or a wrong zone. Range-check inputs and confirm units and datum before trusting any output.

content.html:
Verifying the output

Professional practice never trusts software blindly. Verify with independent checks: inverse between adjusted stations and compare to the field-measured courses; confirm the ratio of closure is plausible for the instrument and method; sanity-check the area against a rough hand estimate; and make sure coordinates are monotonic and reasonable. Confirm whether the program worked in grid or ground and on which datum/zone. Finally, document the software, version, adjustment method, settings, and basis of bearings — the record that lets another professional reproduce and defend the result.

content.text: Texas context: regardless of the software used, the survey must meet TBPELS standards of practice (22 TAC ch. 138) and clearly state the basis of bearings, datum (typically NAD83 Texas State Plane), and whether distances are grid or ground. The licensed surveyor remains responsible for the result, not the software.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Software automates lat/dep, misclosure, ratio, adjustment, coordinates, and area, but depends on correct input.
Compass-rule spreadsheets suit a simple loop; least squares handles redundant, mixed observations rigorously.
Least squares reports error ellipses and statistics describing positional uncertainty.
Always verify output (inverse, closure, area sanity) and confirm grid vs. ground and datum.
The licensed surveyor, not the software, is responsible under TBPELS standards.

flashcards.definition: Coordinate-geometry and survey software that automates traverse computation, adjustment, coordinates, and area from input courses or coordinates.
Programs that weight redundant observations by precision to produce optimal coordinates plus error ellipses and statistics.
A graphical statistical region from a least-squares adjustment showing the positional uncertainty of a computed station.

lessons.title: Week 15: Professional Standards and Comprehensive Review
learning_objectives: Summarize the Texas professional standards governing land surveying practice.
Synthesize the course: measurement, adjustment, COGO, area, and boundary determination.
Prepare for the final exam by reviewing the full traverse-to-boundary workflow.
key_takeaways: Texas surveying is regulated by TBPELS under Tex. Occ. Code ch. 1071, with standards of practice in 22 TAC ch. 138.
The course arc: measure (distance/angle) -> check and adjust (misclosure, compass/transit) -> compute (coordinates, area) -> determine the boundary (evidence, dignity of calls, footsteps).
Boundary location is governed by intent, monuments, and the original surveyor's footsteps, not by recomputed measurement alone.
Professional duties include accuracy standards, proper records, basis of bearings, and continuing competence.

content.html:
Texas professional standards

Land surveying in Texas is a licensed profession regulated by the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS) under the Professional Land Surveying Practices Act, Tex. Occ. Code ch. 1071. The Board's rules live in 22 Texas Administrative Code, Title 22, Part 6, with the surveyor standards of practice and professional conduct in ch. 138. These standards govern who may practice, the categories and required accuracies of surveys, the contents of a survey and plat, the basis of bearings and datum, recordkeeping, and ethical duties. A survey that does not meet the applicable category's standards is professionally deficient regardless of how precise its arithmetic looks.

Tying the course together

SRVY 1341 is one connected story. You learned to measure distances (with tape corrections) and directions (compass, declination, angle types); to set up and care for instruments; to resolve courses into latitudes and departures, quantify misclosure and the ratio of closure, and adjust by the compass or transit rule; to apply coordinate geometry (inverse, intersections) and compute area (coordinate and DMD methods); and finally to determine boundaries from evidence using the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine, including subdivision and curve concepts. Each topic feeds the next: good measurement enables a tight traverse, a closed traverse yields trustworthy coordinates and area, and sound evidence analysis turns those numbers into a defensible boundary.

content.text: Final-exam focus: be able to run a full closed traverse end to end and to reason about a boundary-evidence scenario using the dignity of calls and footsteps doctrine. Those two abilities span most of the course.

Texas context: remember the Texas distinctives throughout - the GLO original-survey, metes-and-bounds system (no PLSS grid); varas in historic records; the dignity-of-calls hierarchy from Stafford v. King; footsteps over recomputed measurement; and TBPELS regulation under Tex. Occ. Code ch. 1071 and 22 TAC ch. 138.

content.html:
Scope of the final

The final exam is comprehensive, spanning the whole course. Computationally, expect a full closed-traverse problem (latitudes and departures, misclosure, ratio of closure, compass/transit adjustment, coordinates, and area), plus coordinate geometry (inverse and intersections), area by coordinate and DMD methods, and curve elements (T, L, C from R and Δ). Conceptually, expect boundary-determination questions: dignity of calls, original vs. lost corners, senior/junior rights, the footsteps doctrine, subdivision concepts, and Texas professional standards.

How to perform

Bring the disciplined habits the course built. For every computation, write the formula, substitute with units, evaluate, and run the built-in check (hypot recombination for a course, sums-to-zero for an adjusted traverse, inverse to verify a course, a rough area estimate). For boundary-reasoning questions, anchor your answer in the controlling principle: intent first, then the dignity of calls, then the footsteps of the original surveyor — and flag the Texas distinctives (GLO/metes-and-bounds, no PLSS, varas, Stafford v. King). Manage time: secure the parts you know, show steps for partial credit, and do not let one hard item consume the hour.
